title: Public Works Committee
overview: The Public Works Committee approved several key items including a resolution supporting state transportation funding, awarded a $126,900 recycled asphalt crushing contract to King Gravel, and directed staff to develop enforcement language for sanitary sewer discharge violations at $100/day. The committee also approved construction agreements for multiple road projects and utility connections.

agenda.item: Acknowledge April 2026 Water and Sewer Permits
Review 2025 Weston Water Utility PSC Annual Report

discussions.body: Michael reported that the village received a local road improvement grant for Birch Street reconstruction between Schofield Avenue and Community Center Drive, originally a 2029 project now likely moving to 2028. The grant covers over 50% of the approximately $1.5 million project. Michael noted the village has received over $9 million in grant funding over the past 4-5 years. Discussion included Weston Avenue repairs by Integrity Grading and Excavating for road depression issues over the sanitary sewer trench, with understanding repairs may need to continue if settlement recurs.

Staff reported on spring operations including road limit removal on April 29th, mailbox and sign repairs, and dewatering operations near Weston Avenue tributary for approximately one week. Discussion addressed flooding issues in the Crane Meadows area with potential permanent pump solutions being explored. Crews completed curb line sweeping, hot mix patching, storm damage cleanup from East Nick area, and inlet repairs before Birch Street reconstruction.

Josh reported on water service repair at Caribou Coffee, well maintenance items, and sanitary sewer issues including pump maintenance and power failures at lift stations. Significant discussion focused on clear water infiltration - in April, 37 million gallons entered the system but 53-54 million gallons were treated, indicating approximately 15 million gallons of clear water from infiltration or illegal sump pump connections. The committee discussed the upcoming INI study by In-Pipe Technologies to identify problem areas.

Michael presented the annual Public Service Commission report showing net operating income of $482,911 for 2025, consistent with 2024's $490,000. Water loss was 6%. The utility value increased to nearly $34 million with $6.7 million in infrastructure placed in service. The utility has approximately 6,100 total customers with 5,250 in Weston and 830 in Rothschild. Motion to acknowledge passed unanimously.

Michael explained this League of Wisconsin Municipalities sample resolution supports long-term funding solutions for state transportation. The resolution addresses the need for recurring annual funding for street maintenance and snow plowing rather than just one-time grant funding. The village has decreased its public works budget recently. Motion to recommend adoption passed unanimously.

Michael presented the recycling program at the Ryan Street site where concrete and asphalt are crushed into base course. King Gravel submitted the low bid at $4.23/ton for an estimated 30,000 tons totaling $126,900 (two cents lower than the next bidder at $4.25). Analysis showed this saves approximately $11 per cubic yard compared to City of Wausau costs, representing about $130,000 savings across three 2026 projects. Motion to award to King Gravel passed unanimously.

Staff sought guidance on enforcing violations for illegal sump pump connections to sanitary sewers. Current code allows fines of $100-$1,000 per day. Committee discussed distinguishing between unknowing violations (corrected with notice) versus willful violations. Trustee Zagami recommended $100/day starting point, noting it adds up quickly. Staff will bring cost analysis of treatment expenses to future meeting. Motion to direct staff to create enforcement language at $100/day maximum passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Water and Sewer Connection Agreement - Radkey Avenue
discussions.body: Michael presented an agreement for a duplex at 2230 Radkey Avenue to temporarily connect via long lateral until Radkey Avenue reconstruction when mains will be extended. Committee directed adding specific date language that reconstruction occur no later than 2029. Motion passed unanimously with amendment.

The committee approved an intergovernmental agreement with Village of Rothschild for Military Road construction from Wolfman Street to Helin Street. Weston's share for sanitary sewer ($6,156) and water main ($88,704) totals $94,860. Steam Construction is the contractor. Motion passed unanimously.

Michael presented proposals for construction inspection services. For Alderson/Jelinek intersection project, MSA bid $37,315 versus Clark Dietz at $48,910 - MSA recommended. For Bladel Avenue, MSA bid $70,400 versus Clark Dietz at $73,730 - Clark Dietz recommended due to having full-time experienced staff rather than summer intern. Motion to approve both recommendations passed unanimously.

actionItems: April 13, 2026 meeting minutes approved
April 2026 water and sewer permits acknowledged
2025 Water Utility PSC Annual Report acknowledged
Resolution 2026-014 supporting state transportation funding recommended for board adoption
Recycled asphalt crushing contract awarded to King Gravel for $126,900 (estimated)
Staff directed to create enforcement language for sanitary sewer discharge violations at $100/day
Water/sewer connection agreement for 2230 Radkey Avenue approved with 2029 reconstruction deadline
Military Road construction agreement with Rothschild approved at $94,860 village share
MSA approved for Alderson/Jelinek construction services at $37,315
Clark Dietz approved for Bladel Avenue construction services at $73,730
Special assessment meeting scheduled for May 28, 2026 at 6:00 PM for Bladel Avenue project
Norcon to begin Schofield Avenue concrete repairs starting mid-week

title: Tourism Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Tourism Commission held its reorganization meeting, electing new officers and approving a $5,000 grant for the Taste and Glow Balloonfest. The commission also received a report on the Alice in Dairyland event recently hosted in Marathon County.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
New business - Grant request: Taste and Glow Balloonfest
Remarks from chair and future items

discussions.body: The commission elected officers for the reorganized commission. Trustee Erling was nominated for chair and approved unanimously. Cushing was nominated for vice chair by motion, seconded by Jackson, and approved unanimously. Renee was nominated and approved unanimously as secretary.
The minutes from the April 21st meeting were approved. Motion was made and seconded by Renee, passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Grant Request - Taste and Glow Balloonfest
discussions.body: Brandon Jill (3308 Green Pastures Lane) presented the grant request for the Taste and Glow Balloonfest. He reported attendance of 11,000-12,000 last year and noted a competing Marshfield event has been canceled due to permit issues, making them the sole hot air balloon event in the area. Commission members questioned room night tracking, noting the application showed 20 fewer rooms than last year. The commission requested better room tracking data from hotels for future applications. Motion to approve $5,000 grant (same as last year) was made by Erling, seconded by Jackson, and passed unanimously.

Renee reported on attending the Alice in Dairyland dinner at Red Rock Orchard in Edgar. She noted the candidates visited various Marathon County locations including Mullen's Cheese, and that Anastasia won the competition. This was the first time Marathon County hosted the event since the 1970s.

actionItems: Approved $5,000 grant for Taste and Glow Balloonfest
Taste and Glow Balloonfest organizers to provide hotel room tracking data in post-event report
Next meeting scheduled for July 20th at 4:00 PM (time to be confirmed)

title: Plan Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Plan Commission approved the site plan for the new Mountain Bay Metropolitan Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive, and adopted a new salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection districts. Both items passed unanimously with all commissioners present voting in favor.

agenda.item: Call to order and roll call
Site plan review for Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive
Salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection district
April 2026 staff approved certified survey maps and site plans
Planning and development department monthly project update
Future agenda items and staff comments

discussions.item: Site plan review for Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility
discussions.body: Staff presented the site plan for the new police department and municipal court at 3600 Community Center Drive. The building was rotated to address challenges with a gas pipeline running under the parking lot and to reduce fill costs. Chief Jeremy explained the building has capacity for 50-60 officers versus current staffing of 40, with expansion possible to the north. An alternate storage shed is proposed but may be deferred based on bid costs. Commissioner Garant made the motion with conditions 2-5, which passed unanimously. Discussion included questions about future expandability and positioning of the alternate shed to accommodate future growth.

discussions.item: Salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection district
discussions.body: Jennifer presented a new inspection program requiring two annual inspections per salt storage facility in wellhead protection areas - one warm weather inspection focusing on structural integrity and one winter operational inspection. A standardized inspection worksheet similar to DOT's format will be used. Staff identified one existing facility near Sternberg that predates the wellhead protection ordinance and has never gone through the conditional use process. The commission agreed staff should reach out to bring them into compliance. Motion to approve passed unanimously with amendment to report back to both Plan Commission and Public Works committee.
Motion to approve minutes as presented was made, seconded, and passed unanimously with no discussion.

discussions.item: April 2026 staff approved certified survey maps and site plans
discussions.body: Approved unanimously with no discussion.
Approved unanimously with no discussion.

discussions.item: Planning and development department monthly project update
discussions.body: Jennifer announced the first joint Plan Commission and Village Board steering committee meeting for the comprehensive plan is scheduled for Monday, June 1st at 5:00 p.m. She will send email and calendar invites to members.

publicComment: Jim Pensel of 5002 Arrow Street, Weston spoke via online participation. He urged the commission to exercise common sense regarding landscaping requirements for the police department project, referencing relaxed standards applied to the Green Heck Turner building. He also requested the commission require annual reporting on the salt shed inspection program to track compliance over time.
actionItems: Site plan for Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive approved with staff conditions 2-5
Salt storage shed inspection program adopted for wellhead protection district with two annual inspections per facility
Staff to report back on salt shed inspection program to both Plan Commission and Public Works committee next year
Staff to reach out to existing salt storage facility on Sternberg that has not gone through wellhead protection conditional use process
Joint Plan Commission and Village Board steering committee meeting for comprehensive plan scheduled for June 1st at 5:00 p.m.
Staff to send presentation on residential TIF district qualifications to commission members

title: Health & Human Services Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Health & Human Services Committee held an orientation meeting for new board members, featuring educational presentations from the Health Department, Social Services, Veteran Services, and the Aging & Disability Resource Center. No action items were voted on beyond approving April meeting minutes. The committee learned about department structures, budgets, and current initiatives including homelessness response, veteran suicide prevention, and challenges facing nutrition programs.

agenda.item: Call to order and notation of remote attendance (Supervisors Kremes and Vice Chair Rosenberg)
Approval of April 1st committee meeting minutes
Operations functions required by statute - no items this meeting
Educational presentations - Review of standing committee ordinance section 2.04

discussions.body: Supervisor Cavelli made a motion to approve the minutes, seconded by Vice Chair Rosenberg. The motion carried unanimously with no opposition.

Administrator Lance Leonard explained the committee's structure under ordinance section 2.04 subsection 5, covering jurisdiction over the Health Department, Social Services, Veteran Services, North Central Health Care, and the Aging & Disability Resource Center. He outlined affiliated boards including the Social Services Board, Veteran Service Commission, Transportation Coordinating Committee, and Board of Health. Chair Boots noted the committee will be working on a new strategic plan with a target completion of December.

Health Officer Caitlyn Suginaka presented the department's mission and structure, highlighting 37.6 FTE staff across environmental health, nursing, and community health improvement teams. She discussed public health vending machines offering free supplies like tick kits, the Nurse Family Partnership program with first graduates, and the new homeless system response position created through a task force last session. Administrator Leonard noted about half of the department's $5 million budget comes from tax levy, with significant grant and fee revenue.

Administrator Leonard presented on behalf of Director Christa, covering the department's 140 FTE staff and $22 million budget with approximately $7 million in tax levy. He highlighted the successful Family Keys pilot program addressing housing instability for child welfare and youth justice families, noting many participants had never had a bank account or formal lease. He mentioned potential upcoming requests for restructuring including converting lead worker positions to supervisory roles.

Veteran Service Officer Aaron Galindo reported the department now has 3.0 FTE and had 8,149 interactions in 2025. He highlighted $44 million in annual VA compensation flowing to county veterans and $2.6 million in retroactive payments in 2025. Key initiatives include surviving spouse benefits outreach, housing stability partnerships with Center for Veterans Issues, and suicide prevention efforts noting 7 of Marathon County's 30 suicides in 2025 were veterans. Supervisor Kremes asked about regional coordination, and Galindo confirmed good working relationships with neighboring counties while noting state statute requires each county to have its own VSO.

Executive Director Mike Ray explained the four-county regional organization serving Marathon, Lincoln, Langlade, and Wood counties with about 60 FTE and 385-400 volunteers. He discussed financial challenges with nutrition program costs up 40-45% over four years while federal funding remained flat. The organization hadn't requested a levy increase from 2011 until 2024. Marathon County's contribution is $413,135. Vice Chair Rosenberg thanked the program for culturally appropriate meals served at the Hmong American Center.
Vice Chair Rosenberg caught an error in the announced next meeting date. The correct date was confirmed as Wednesday, June 3rd at 3 PM, not May 29th which is not a Wednesday.

actionItems: April 1st meeting minutes approved
North Central Healthcare to be scheduled for separate full presentation before committee
Chair Boots to bring strategic plan discussion to upcoming meetings
Committee members invited to submit agenda items via email to Chair Boots and Administrator Leonard
Next meeting scheduled for Wednesday, June 3rd at 3 PM

title: Board of Trustees
overview: The Village of Weston Board of Trustees approved increased park and recreation impact fees, passed several ordinances including mobile food vendor licensing and e-bicycle regulations, and discussed ongoing concerns about the farmers market relocation to Kennedy Park. The board also adjourned the Board of Review to June 15, 2026 and made appointments to the CLIPS committee.

agenda.item: Board of Review called to order and adjourned to June 15, 2026
Board of Trustees meeting called to order, roll call
Public hearing on park and recreation impact fees (Ordinance 26-010 and Resolution 2026-015)
Approval of minutes from previous meetings
Acknowledge reports from boards, committees, and commissions with chair updates
Consent agenda - vouchers and license renewals
Ordinances including mobile food vendor, nuisances, e-bicycles, and transportation funding resolution
Discussion on farmers market location at Kennedy Park

discussions.item: Public Hearing - Park and Recreation Impact Fees
discussions.body: Staff presented a proposed increase to park and recreation impact fees from $300 per single family unit to $761, $250 per duplex to $563, and multifamily to $495, based on the 2022 public facilities needs assessment. Jim Pitel spoke during public comment questioning the justification for the numbers without detailed park maintenance cost data. Trustee Motti supported the fee increase, noting population growth requires more park space. Ordinance 26-010 was approved with motion by Lope Sorrell, second by Zagami, passed unanimously. Resolution 2026-015 was approved with motion by Hermling, second by Clark, passed unanimously.

The board debated amendments to the remote meeting attendance policy. Discussion centered on removing specific justification requirements for remote attendance. Trustee Beck moved to amend the policy to strike sentences requiring health, safety, or welfare justifications, keeping only the requirements to notify officials 12 hours in advance, be on camera, and not participate in closed sessions. The amendment passed with two no votes from Trustees Hermling and Motti. The original motion as amended also passed with the same two dissenting votes.

The president appointed Stephanie Daniels (currently on Finance and HR) and Golden Mua to the CLIPS committee. Motion by Hermling, second by Lopes, approved unanimously. The board discussed having committee applications shared in the packet for transparency. Openings remain on Parks, Finance/HR, and one more on CLIPS.

Chief Finkey explained the ordinance creates a regional reciprocity system where food trucks inspected by one participating municipality (including Marshfield, Wisconsin Rapids, Stevens Point, and others) receive a sticker valid across all participating communities, eliminating redundant inspections. Ordinance 26-008 was approved unanimously.

Board members discussed ongoing concerns about the farmers market's new location at Kennedy Park after losing their previous site to development. Trustee Beck raised safety concerns about cars parking on grass along Alderson and within the park. Renee explained the market has grown from 25 to 53 vendor spots and vendors now appreciate having vehicles with them. The board acknowledged if no suitable location can be found, they may need to suspend the market temporarily. No action was taken; this was discussion only.

The board approved a secondhand article and jewelry dealer license for Scoffield Coin and Hobby LLC at 5703 Jamar Street. Chief Hunt explained these licenses protect against stolen merchandise sales. The motion was amended to require satisfactory background check completion and receipt of occupancy permit, as the building is still under construction. Approved unanimously as amended.

Public Works Director Michael Dolan explained the need for outside engineering consultants ($108,000 estimated) for project oversight, noting difficulty filling an engineering technician position due to limited workforce in central Wisconsin. He mentioned even consulting firms are losing employees to relocation to southern Wisconsin.

publicComment: Jim Pitel of 52 Street spoke during public comment criticizing the board for hiring outside engineering consultants when an engineering technician position remains unfilled, questioning why barriers to filling the position haven't been shared with the board. He also criticized the appointment process for committees, noting Lisa Beck was introduced at a SAFER meeting before being officially appointed. Pitel complained about lack of detailed Kennedy Park information in the packet and called for better accountability from the administrator.
actionItems: Park and recreation impact fees increased effective with Ordinance 26-010 and Resolution 2026-015
Board of Review adjourned to June 15, 2026
Comprehensive Plan Steering Committee meeting scheduled for Monday, June 1st at 6:00 PM (joint meeting with Plan Commission)
Staff to prepare intergovernmental service agreement for Ringle building inspection assistance
Weston Avenue speed limit study to be completed and brought back to board
Committee applications to be included in board packets going forward
Stephanie Daniels and Golden Mua appointed to CLIPS committee
Secondhand dealer license approved for Scoffield Coin and Hobby LLC contingent on background check and occupancy permit
Mobile food vendor regional reciprocity ordinance approved
Remote meeting attendance policy amended to remove specific justification requirements

title: Regular Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Board held an organizational meeting where committee appointments were approved, two at-large executive committee members were elected (Supervisors Hapa and Arstead), and a resolution was unanimously passed to reduce landfill tipping fees for debris related to the state-declared tornado and flood emergency affecting Ringle and other areas. The board suspended its rules to consider the emergency fee reduction, which allows affected residents to pay $36.94 per ton instead of the standard $65 rate through June 17th.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance and moment of reflection
Reading of the notice and roll call
Committee appointments by Chair and Administrator
Election of at-large executive committee members
Consent agenda items C10 through C14A
Resolution 24-26: Out of home placement reserve increase
Resolution 26-26: Landfill tipping fee modification for storm debris
Motion to suspend rules for tipping fee resolution

discussions.body: Chair Gibbs outlined goals for committee appointments including increasing diversity of perspective and ensuring supervisor development opportunities. Vice Chair Boots assisted in developing the appointments. The motion was made by Supervisor Cavelli and seconded by Supervisor Crims. The motion carried but was not unanimous. Supervisor Rosenberg noted the system marked them absent without giving them an option to vote.

discussions.item: Election of at-large executive committee members
discussions.body: Three supervisors were nominated: Supervisor Hapa (nominated by Supervisor Mask), Supervisor Leur (nominated by Supervisor Rosenberg), and Supervisor Arstead (nominated by Vice Chair Boots). Supervisor Rosenberg explained she nominated Supervisor Leur because she had observed her advocating for the county's youngest and oldest residents. Paper ballots were distributed and supervisors selected two names. Supervisors Hapa and Arstead were elected to the executive committee.
Items C10 through C14A were approved on consent. Motion by Supervisor Cavelli, seconded by Supervisor Drebeck. The motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Resolution 24-26: Out of home placement reserve increase
discussions.body: Motion by Supervisor Morash, seconded by Supervisor Robinson. No discussion was offered. The motion carried but was not unanimous.
Motion by Supervisor P, seconded by Supervisor Robinson. No discussion was offered. The motion carried but was not unanimous.

Administrator Leonard and Director Hagen Busher presented the proposal from the Solid Waste Management Board to reduce tipping fees for storm-related debris from the standard $65/ton to $36.94/ton for Ringle residents (or $39.27 for other areas). The state waived $13.10 and the town of Ringle waived $2.33. Leonard noted Marathon County likely won't meet the $670,743 threshold for state disaster aid but expects Ringle will qualify. He mentioned exploring partnership with the Community Foundation to help residents with unreimbursed costs. After public comment from Jessica Toasty and Matt Haybeck requesting the county wave all fees, and extensive board discussion about timing, FEMA implications, and tracking costs, the board unanimously suspended its rules to consider the resolution directly. The resolution was then approved unanimously with motion by Supervisor Arstead and second by Supervisor Morash.

publicComment: Two residents were granted special permission to speak on the landfill tipping fee resolution. Jessica Toasty requested Marathon County wave all fees, noting the state, DNR, and town of Ringle waived their portions, and that one business has spent $40,000 on cleanup while the community has been largely self-reliant. She asked for a six-month waiver. Matt Haybeck spoke on behalf of residents whose homes need demolition, requesting fee relief for underinsured homeowners and noting some of the approximately 44 affected homes still await insurance adjusters, suggesting more time is needed beyond the 60-day window.
actionItems: Committee appointments approved as presented by Chair Gibbs
Supervisors Hapa and Arstead elected to at-large executive committee positions
Resolution 24-26 approved for out of home placement reserve increase
Resolution 25-26 approved for social services budget amendment
Resolution 26-26 approved reducing landfill tipping fees for storm debris to $36.94/ton through June 17th
Administrator Leonard to continue discussions with Community Foundation about reimbursement mechanism for unreimbursed insurance costs
Solid Waste Management Board to develop long-term policy on handling similar disaster situations within six months
Board to revisit tipping fee extension at May meeting if needed

title: Environmental Resources Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Environmental Resources Committee approved two rezoning petitions unanimously and voted to forward a modified fee waiver resolution to the county board that will only waive reconnect fees for properties impacted by the April 17, 2026 tornado. The committee also received educational presentations from Solid Waste, Parks Recreation and Forestry, and Conservation Planning and Zoning departments.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Public hearing - Tim Veland rezoning petition (urban residential/general agriculture to rural residential, Town of Green Valley)
Public hearing - Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition (Conservancy Recreation to General Agriculture, Town of McMillan)
Consideration of fee waiver for properties impacted by April 17, 2026 severe weather
Educational presentation - Solid Waste Department overview
Educational presentation - Parks, Recreation and Forestry Department overview
Educational presentation - Conservation Planning and Zoning Department overview

discussions.body: Supervisor Sefeld made a motion to approve the minutes, seconded by Supervisor Drebeck. The motion carried unanimously with no discussion.

discussions.item: Tim Veland rezoning petition - Town of Green Valley
discussions.body: Garrett Pogle presented the staff report for rezoning from urban residential and general agriculture to rural residential for a land division. The Town of Green Valley recommended approval on March 10, 2026. No public testimony was offered. Supervisor Drebeck moved to approve based on findings of fact and town approval, seconded by Supervisor Kurt. Motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition - Town of McMillan
discussions.body: Staff presented the petition to rezone from Conservancy Recreation to General Agriculture. Supervisor Leur asked about the conservancy nature, and Garrett Pogle explained the property has a home and horse riding stable that are non-conforming uses in conservancy, and rezoning would make them conforming. He confirmed no natural area would be lost. Town of McMillan recommended approval on April 20, 2026. Supervisor Sefeld moved to approve, seconded by Supervisor Drew. Motion carried unanimously.

Director Lori Mskimmons presented a proposal to waive county zoning and private on-site wastewater treatment system fees for properties impacted by the April 17, 2026 severe weather. Supervisor Drew questioned where to draw the line for future events. Supervisor Leur asked about tracking costs. Supervisor Drebeck expressed concerns about fairness to others not in declared disaster areas and questioned consistency with recent fee increases. Supervisor Sefeld moved to forward the resolution, seconded by Supervisor Leur. Supervisor Drebeck then moved to amend the resolution to only waive reconnect fees ($160) and no other pouts or zoning fees, seconded by Supervisor Kurt. The amendment passed. The amended motion to forward to county board passed but was not unanimous.

actionItems: Forward amended fee waiver resolution to county board - waiving only reconnect fees ($160) for tornado-impacted properties
Rezoning for James Offer and David Tract property forwarded to county board for approval
Rezoning for Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz property forwarded to county board for approval
Staff to track costs and requests related to fee waivers for future policy development

title: Parks Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Parks Committee approved installation of new kayak launch signs along the Plover River, deferred discussion on dog days at the aquatic center to next month, and received updates on Kennedy Park fundraising and Yellow Banks disc golf course improvements. Public commenters criticized the meeting time and questioned Kennedy Park financial transparency.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Yellow Banks disc golf course update
Dog Day at the aquatic center
Graffiti on the Oaklair River Trail
Topics for future meetings and staff remarks

discussions.body: Minutes from the April 27th, 2026 meeting were approved. A motion was made and seconded with no discussion. The motion carried unanimously with all present voting in favor.

Staff presented a timeline document showing the project history from the comprehensive outdoor recreation plan approval in November 2023 through the May 9th ribbon cutting. Committee member Scott asked clarifying questions about village capital dollars and fundraising timelines. Staff explained the Friends of Kennedy Park are a volunteer nonprofit working toward a 10-year plan, currently fundraising for $117,000 in field lights with approximately $84-85,000 already at the community foundation. Staff noted the Friends group plans to make a public check presentation at their first June tournament.

discussions.item: Yellow Banks disc golf course update
discussions.body: Staff presented photos and a PDF showing approximate locations of trees removed from the river and the installation of wooden mats over gas pipelines to protect them during equipment access. Dan explained the mats are approximately 8x8 posts with notches and lifting rings, and the village has extra mats that may be used on trail projects.

discussions.item: Dog Day at the aquatic center
discussions.body: The committee deferred this item to next month because Committee Chair Katrina was absent due to an unforeseen issue. A public commenter named Andy (6504 [street name inaudible]) spoke against allowing dogs in the public pool, stating 'There should not be animals allowed into a public pool. Period.' The chair noted Andy's objection would be recorded.

discussions.item: Graffiti on the Oaklair River Trail
discussions.body: Staff explained challenges removing graffiti from concrete and benches, noting the pressure washer cannot access the trail and benches weigh 2,000 pounds each. They discussed possible solutions including anti-vandalism coatings and seal coats. Committee members suggested volunteer work days, noting community members love the trail. No formal action was taken; staff will investigate cleaning options.

Dan from Marathon County (2625 Northwestern Avenue) presented version seven of signs for kayak launches along the Plover River. Signs will be installed at Highway J, Ross Avenue, and Yellow Banks within Weston, plus locations in Schofield. The signs include a QR code to the river gauge site and donation URL to Marathon County Parks Foundation. The committee approved a motion to allow the village to install the signs, which will go to the village board. Motion carried unanimously.

Staff asked about interest in food allergy awareness signage at playgrounds, noting an individual offered to provide signs encouraging keeping food at picnic tables rather than play equipment. The committee agreed to add this as a future agenda item for further discussion.

publicComment: Two public comments were received. Andy (6504 [street name inaudible]) spoke in person opposing dogs at the aquatic center, stating dogs belong in rivers and lakes, not public pools. Jim Pensan (5002 [street name] Street, Weston), calling in under the name 'Vern M,' criticized the 4 p.m. meeting time as discouraging public participation and questioned Kennedy Park financial transparency, calling the $8,700 expense figure 'too coincidental' and asking about the $10,000 grant and glow event proceeds, suggesting the Friends of Kennedy Park may be 'frauds' for not sharing detailed finances.
actionItems: Kayak launch signs approved for installation at Yellow Banks, Ross Avenue, and Highway J locations - to be forwarded to village board
Dog Day at aquatic center discussion deferred to June meeting
Staff to investigate graffiti removal options and possible protective coatings for Oaklair River Trail amenities
Food allergy awareness signage to be added as future agenda item
Mock Miller Park master plan process to begin with Rettler, kickoff meeting scheduled for following Tuesday
Next meeting scheduled for June 22nd, 2026

title: Finance and Human Resources Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Finance and Human Resources Committee approved previous meeting minutes and acknowledged April budget and financial reports. The meeting featured an extensive educational presentation from Municipal Clerk Pam on the clerk's office operations, elections, licensing, and budget. Committee member Evan Ewert announced his resignation as he is moving out of Weston.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of minutes from previous Finance and HR meeting
TIF one and TIF two detail reports for April
Educational presentation - Municipal Clerk's budget and office overview
Staff remarks and farmers market update

discussions.item: Approval of minutes from previous Finance and HR meeting
discussions.body: Motion to approve minutes was made by Ernling and seconded by Daniels. The motion passed unanimously with no discussion.

discussions.item: TIF one and TIF two detail reports for April
discussions.body: A committee member asked about the final numbers for TIF closure. Finance Director Jessica explained they are anticipating October/November for bond payoff, with bonds due October 1st. The CDA will need to pass a resolution to call the bonds this summer. Jessica confirmed the village will be able to pay off the debt as planned.

discussions.item: Acknowledgment of work product transmittals (items 5, 6, and 7)
discussions.body: Motion to acknowledge the April budget status report, TIF detail reports, and legal details report was made by Daniels and seconded by Soaper. The motion passed unanimously.

Clerk Pam presented an overview of her department's operations including elections, licensing, tax collection, and pet licensing. She noted the office went from four full-time staff to currently two full-time and one part-time employee. She highlighted that 84% ($22 million) of tax collection is handled through walk-ins at the clerk's office. Discussion included the pet licensing fee increase from $10 to $20 for neutered dogs, which was driven by Marathon County raising fees to cover their $100,000 annual payment to the Humane Society. The committee discussed the cost of the quarterly newsletter 'The Wire' at approximately $30,000 annually for 5,089 postal patrons, with some questioning whether alternatives should be explored.

Staff member Renee provided an update on the farmers market at Kennedy Park. She reported attending the last three Saturdays for 6-7 hours each. The market has expanded to include coffee vendors, new food trucks, and craft vendors. Vendors expressed satisfaction with the new setup and location. Committee members raised concerns about parking congestion on Alderson and Newport streets, with staff clarifying parking is restricted on the east side of Alderson but allowed on the west side and both sides of Newport.

Committee member Evan Ewert announced he is stepping down from the committee as he and his wife are moving away from Weston, though staying in the metro area. This was his last meeting. The chair noted there are now open spots on this and other committees and encouraged members to recruit interested citizens.

actionItems: CDA to pass resolution to call TIF bonds this summer for October 1st payoff
Recruit new committee members to fill vacancy created by Evan Ewert's resignation
Next meeting scheduled for Monday, June 15th at 4:30 PM

title: Community Life and Public Safety
overview: The Village of Weston Community Life and Public Safety Committee met on May 4th, taking action on several ordinance amendments and property matters. Key decisions included recommending approval of nuisance code amendments, an e-bike ordinance with added e-moto restrictions, mobile food vendor fire inspection requirements, address corrections, and a raze order for an abandoned structure on Shore Avenue.

agenda.item: Call to order and pledge of allegiance
Approval of minutes from previous meeting
Mobile food vendors fire inspection ordinance

discussions.body: Jim Senalt spoke critically about the new farmers market location at a park, predicting the grass will be destroyed by vehicles parking on it. He questioned whether current village code allows parking on grass and raised concerns about ADA-compliant handicap parking being placed on grass rather than asphalt. He also questioned the new fire inspection requirement for food trucks, asking where it came from, and criticized the age of some code violation cases on the enforcement list.

Staff reported that 3020 Mountain View Avenue will be removed from the code enforcement list after the property sold at auction and the new owner submitted repair permits. Chief Hunt reported the police department had 2,394 calls in March (77 per day), 410 traffic stops, and 281 citations. The department has hired its 40th officer, with the 38th starting last week, 39th in May, and 40th around January. Two SROs work with zoning violations during summer. Motion to acknowledge reports 5, 6, and 7 passed unanimously.

The committee reviewed final amendments including switching tree limb clearance requirements to 14 and 10 feet over streets and walkways. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Campkey, to recommend approval to the Board of Trustees passed unanimously.

Committee discussed the model e-bike ordinance for Chapter 82. Chief Hunt strongly advocated for prohibiting e-motos (electric dirt bikes) from roadways and sidewalks, comparing them to unlicensed motorcycles that pose safety hazards. Discussion ensued about electric scooters used by middle schoolers, with the committee deciding to leave officer discretion for kids' play scooters. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Chwanka, to recommend approval with the addition of e-motos being prohibited on roadways, sidewalks, trails, and public property passed unanimously.

Trustee Tolson presented research showing Weston's 12-hour parking restriction (4am-4pm) is far outside norms of surrounding municipalities, most having 5-hour windows. Michael (DPW) suggested eliminating overnight parking restrictions and instead signing the 6-8 problem areas, mostly near apartment complexes. Chief Hunt preferred keeping overnight restrictions to give officers time to clear vehicles before plowing. The committee decided to table the item until July for Michael to identify specific exception areas.

Mount Olive Church requested a crosswalk between the church and DC Everest parking area. Committee members noted no accidents have occurred and questioned costs. Michael estimated $2,000-3,000 for basic crosswalk, $25,000-30,000 for lighted system. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Chwanka, to recommend board approval contingent on Mount Olive covering installation costs passed unanimously.

Chief Hunt explained a regional partnership between fire departments (Wisconsin Rapids, Marshfield, Stevens Point, Plover, Rib Mountain, and Safer) for food truck inspections. One inspection with a sticker covers all participating municipalities. The city of Wausau opted out to do their own. Fire Marshal Marty recently failed one truck with galvanized steel LP piping and unsecured propane tanks. Motion by Chwanka, seconded by Campkey, to approve Ordinance 26-8 passed unanimously.

Staff presented three addressing errors: 5915 Delicoski Street needs to become 5915/5917 for a duplex; Ross Avenue addresses need resequencing due to a discovered apartment unit; and 2211 Radkey Avenue needs correction for a new duplex on a private drive. Motion to recommend approval passed unanimously.

Staff requested authorization for a raze order on an abandoned structure. The property has been abandoned since 2010-2011, with the structure previously used as a chicken coop. A fire department training burn was deemed unsafe due to building condition. Current owners prefer the village proceed with demolition; costs will be placed on tax roll. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Campkey, passed unanimously.

publicComment: Jim Senalt spoke during public comment, criticizing the new farmers market location execution, questioning ADA compliance of handicap parking on grass, asking about the origin of new food truck fire inspections, and expressing concern about the age of pending code violation cases.
actionItems: Recommend Chapter 50 nuisance amendments to Board of Trustees
Recommend e-bike ordinance with e-moto prohibition to Board of Trustees
Michael (DPW) to identify specific areas needing signed parking restrictions for July meeting
Michael to contact Mount Olive Church about covering crosswalk installation costs
Approve Ordinance 26-8 for mobile food vendor fire inspections
Recommend address corrections to Board of Trustees
Authorize raze order proceedings for 4104 Shore Avenue
Staff to investigate property at Sternberg and South Timber with furniture and dumpster outside

title: Committee of the Whole Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education's Committee of the Whole meeting on May 18, 2026 was scheduled to address multiple action items including the preliminary 2026-27 budget, employee handbook updates, meal price changes, and policy revisions. The board was also expected to consider approval of the Riverview HVAC and roofing project and updates to the activities and athletics code.

agenda.item: Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling (Action Requested)
Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code (Action Requested)

discussions.item: Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Thomas Jefferson Elementary as part of the Excellence in Action program. No additional details or presenter information were provided in the agenda.

Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present the Employee Handbook updates for the 2026-27 school year in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to review and consider approval of the updated handbook.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the preliminary budget for 2026-27 in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider the initial budget recommendation for the upcoming school year.
Karen Fochs was scheduled to present proposed changes to the 2026-2027 paid meal prices in an estimated 5 minutes. Supporting documentation included a meal price action sheet and PLE tool.

Dr. Jon Tomski and BJ Brandt were scheduled to present suggested changes to the Athletic and Activities Code in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the 2026-2027 Code of Conduct updates.

discussions.item: Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling
discussions.body: Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present a proposed change to Policy 9270 regarding Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling in an estimated 5 minutes. Action was requested for this policy update.

Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present proposed changes to Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of these policy revisions.

Ryan Urmanski, Director of Buildings and Grounds, was scheduled to present the Riverview HVAC and roofing project in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the RFP for this facility improvement project.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to vote on Employee Handbook Updates for 2026-27
Board was expected to vote on the Preliminary 2026-27 Budget recommendation
Board was expected to vote on 2026-2027 Paid Meal Price Update
Board was expected to vote on Activities & Athletics Code changes
Board was expected to vote on Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling revisions
Board was expected to vote on Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code changes
Board was expected to vote on Riverview RFP Approval for HVAC and roofing project

title: Special Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, this Special Meeting of the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address potential litigation in closed session under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). The board was expected to hold preliminary discussions regarding a legal matter before potentially reconvening in open session to take any necessary action.

agenda.item: Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes
Reconvene in Open Session, to take further action if necessary and appropriate

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to enter closed session to hold preliminary discussions regarding potential litigation, as permitted under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). This statute allows governmental bodies to convene in closed session when considering matters that could result in legal action against or on behalf of the district.
Following the closed session discussion, the board was expected to reconvene in open session to take any further action deemed necessary and appropriate based on the closed session deliberations.

publicComment: No public comment period was included on this agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to potentially take action following the closed session discussion regarding potential litigation

title: Regular Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address several significant action items at their May 11, 2026 regular meeting, including approval of the 2026-27 budget reconciliation plan, teacher contract renewals, and wage increases for non-teaching staff. The board was also expected to consider renewal of the WAMCS charter school contract for 2026-2031 and enter closed session regarding personnel matters including non-renewal notices and contract evaluations.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance: Jim Bouche, President
Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Maine Elementary as part of the district's Excellence in Action program, which typically highlights achievements and initiatives at individual schools.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was expected to present a summary report of all legal counsel expenses incurred during the third quarter of 2025-2026. The report was described as broken down by law firm and by type of legal advice sought, requiring no action as a written report with an estimated 2-minute presentation time.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the budget reconciliation plan for budgeting purposes, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 3 minutes with action requested from the board.
Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present a list of teacher contracts for approval for the 2026-27 school year. The presentation was estimated at 5 minutes with action requested from the board.

Tabatha Gundrum was expected to present a recommendation for a 2.63% wage adjustment for all employee groups in the District except teachers, in accordance with the budget reconciliation plan. The agenda noted that teacher negotiations would commence soon with a separate action at a later date, with this presentation estimated at 5 minutes.

Elizabeth Channel, WAMCS Head of School, was scheduled to present the 2026-2031 contract renewal for the Wausau Area Montessori Charter School, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 2 minutes with action requested.

The board was scheduled to enter closed session pursuant to Wisconsin Statutes sections 19.85(1)(c), (e), and (g) to discuss final notice of non-renewal and contract evaluation matters, with plans to reconvene in open session to take further action if necessary.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to vote on approval of the Consent Agenda including appointments, separations, leaves of absence, retirements, meeting minutes, payment of bills, and donations
Action was requested for the 2026-27 Budget Reconciliation Plan
Board was expected to vote on 2026-27 Teacher Contract Approvals
Action was requested for Various Group Wage/Salary Increase of 2.63% for non-teaching staff
Board was expected to vote on WAMCS Charter School Contract Renewal for 2026-2031
Board was expected to potentially take action following closed session regarding non-renewal notices and contract evaluations

title: Water Works Commission Meeting

The commission also received updates on lead service line replacements and wastewater projects.

The director reported on wastewater projects including the Headworks Screening Project, Cherry Street Lift Station, and Lift Station Assessment with Clark Dietz. This was an informational report with no action required.
The commission approved the 2027 through 2031 Capital Improvement Projects Budget for the Drinking Water and Wastewater Utility. The motion passed.

discussions.item: Sole Source Request for MCEP9 and K144L-NA Polymers
discussions.body: The commission approved the sole source request for MCEP9 and K144L-NA polymers used to thicken solids in the Belt Filter Presses and Gravity Belt Thickeners. The motion passed.
The commission discussed the final WPDES permit issued with a five-year term. This was a discussion item with no formal action taken.

title: City Council Meeting
overview: The Wausau Common Council approved mayoral appointments to multiple boards and commissions, passed a consent agenda including bike rack request procedures and special assessments for street construction, but saw contentious debate over proposed amendments to council standing rules with multiple failed motions. The council voted 11-0 on consent items while rule suspension votes were divided, with one failing 5-6.

agenda.item: Week of the Young Child Proclamation
Joint Resolution from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Approving the Bike Rack Request Form
Resolution from the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects
Ordinance from the Common Council to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council

Confirming Appointments of the Mayor of the City of Wausau to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board and Ethics Board

discussions.body: The council approved minutes from both the March 24, 2026 and April 14, 2026 regular meetings. The motion passed unanimously 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Tom Neal.

The consent agenda was approved 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Bruce Trueblood. This included the Joint Resolution Approving the Bike Rack Request Form from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and Infrastructure & Facilities Committee, as well as the Resolution Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects.

The council voted to suspend Rule 11(A) regarding referral of ordinances, passing 8-3 with Matt Hoenecke, Lou Larson, and Bruce Trueblood voting no. However, the motion to suspend Rule 21 regarding amending of the rules failed 5-6, with Carol Lukens, Michael Martens, Andrew Wiskowski, Kristin Slonski, Sarah Watson, and Lou Larson voting against.

discussions.item: Ordinance to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council
discussions.body: This item saw extensive procedural debate with multiple votes. A motion to approve as amended failed, as did a motion to refer back to the Rules Review Committee. An amendment by Alder Watson subject to attorney review passed, as did a subsequent amendment by Alder Tierney. An amendment proposed by Alder Neal failed.

The council approved the Mayor's appointments to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board, and Ethics Board. The motion passed.

publicComment: Public comment was included on the agenda both before the business meeting (preregistered citizens) and after the business meeting.

Special assessments levied for 2025 Street Construction Projects
